URL? (URL)
Affiliation (institution(s)) (string or N/A)
Response: NVIDIA Corporation; originally developed by NovodeX AG (Switzerland); acquired by Ageia Technologies in 2004; acquired by NVIDIA in 2008

Metric: Software purpose (string)
Response: High-performance, GPU-accelerated physics engine for simulating rigid body dynamics, soft body dynamics, cloth, fluid, and particle systems; primarily used in video games, robotics simulation, and autonomous vehicle development

Response: Windows, Linux, OS X, Android, other (iOS)

Response: C++, C, other (CUDA for GPU computation; Python bindings available)

Metric: Is there evidence that performance was considered? Performance refers to either speed, storage, or throughput. (yes∗, no)
Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: PhysX began as NovodeX (2001), was commercialized with dedicated PPU hardware by Ageia, then acquired and GPU-integrated by NVIDIA (2008). Open-sourced in 2018 to support robotics and AI research. PhysX 5 merges formerly separate Flex library capabilities. Used as physics backend in NVIDIA Isaac Sim, Unreal Engine, Unity, and many robotics simulators.

Metric: Additional comments? (can cover any metrics you feel are missing, or any other thoughts you have)
Response: Installation via CMake is relatively smooth. The generate_projects.sh script automates much of the configuration. GPU simulation requires an NVIDIA GPU and CUDA Toolkit, but CPU-only mode works on any machine. Documentation is clear and professional.

Response: Build and runtime were completely stable on Linux. No crashes or unexpected behaviour observed. PhysX is a mature engine with decades of production use.

Response: The PxErrorCallback mechanism provides structured error reporting. Invalid simulation parameters produce descriptive error messages. Memory allocation failures are handled gracefully.

Response: High-quality documentation and snippet-based getting-started examples. The API is C++ centric and assumes familiarity with physics engine concepts. NVIDIA Developer Forums are active. Primary audience is game developers and robotics engineers.

Response: Stable, well-documented C++ API used by major game engines (Unreal Engine, Unity) and robotics simulators (Isaac Sim, CoppeliaSim). Python bindings available via omni.physx in NVIDIA Omniverse. Supports GPU and CPU simulation paths for flexibility.
